name: Azorean Portuguese
notes: Azorean Portuguese, spoken across the nine islands of the Azores archipelago; reference point is Terceira Island. Key phonological features: (1) diagnostic raised nasal diphthong [õw̃] for mainland EP [ɐ̃w̃] — e.g. não [nõw̃], mão [mõw̃]; (2) conservative preservation of full unstressed vowels — /e/, /o/ resist reduction that mainland EP applies; (3) archaic distinction between /ʒ/ (as in hoje) and /ʃ/ (as in caixa) maintained without merger or neutralization; (4) cluster simplification: muito → [mũt], outros → [otɾ]; (5) /a/ in unstressed syllables realised as [a] rather than mainland [ɐ]; (6) vowel breaking before nasal consonants produces diphthongs not found in standard EP; (7) inter-island variation: São Miguel has distinct /ɛ/ lowering; Flores retains more archaic forms. Prosodically slower with syllable-timed characteristics, contrasting with mainland stress-timing. Glottolog approximation: azor1234 (placeholder).

ancestors.notes: Standard European Portuguese; Azorean diverges in nasal diphthong raising, vowel reduction resistance, and archaic /ʒ/ preservation

sources.author: Boléo, M.P.
sources.title: Estudos de linguística portuguesa e românica

stress.notes: Acordo Ortográfico 1990 conventions: paroxytone default; written accents override; final -i/-u/-r/-l/-z/-x/-im/-om/-um/-ns and nasal diphthongs attract final stress. Unmarked -em/-am stay paroxytone (homem, falam).
